macro_segments.title: Foreign Objects in Internal Organs: Needles and Stones in the Liver and Gallbladder

The Case of the Date Nut or Gallstone in the Gallbladder: Whether It Came Through the Trachea or Tore the Esophagus
Rav Ashi's Position: Large Objects Can Squeeze Through Tubes Over Time Despite Their Size
The Distinction Between Date Stones and Olive Stones: Olive Stones Are Sharp and May Tear
Rashi and Tosafot Debate: Is This Teaching Limited to Opinions We Do Not Follow?

macro_segments.title: The Lung as a Primary Focus of Trefah Concern: Introduction and Etymology

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's Etymology: The Lung (Reyah) Named for Its Health-Giving Properties
The Question of Whether Lung Benefits Come from Eating It Plain or Only Through Medicinal Preparation
Market Pricing Evidence: Lungs Cost More Than the Whole Duck, Indicating They Require Special Preparation

macro_segments.title: Holes in the Lung Caused by the Shochet: Attribution and Presumption

micro_segments.title: If a Hole Is Found Where the Shochet Handles the Lung, Do We Ascribe It to the Shochet?
Rav Adda Bar Natan vs. Mar Zutra: We Do Ascribe Holes to the Shochet's Handling
Parallel Case: Wolf Taking Organs and Making Holes—Similar Attribution Logic
The Case of Worms in the Lung: Do We Assume the Hole Was Made Before or After Slaughter?

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Lenient Position: Holes Must Reach the Major Tracheal Tube

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Opinion: Only Holes Penetrating into the Major Central Tube Are Problematic
The Tradition Debate: Rabbi Meloch's Report of Rabbi Yehoshua Ben Levi's Position—Does He Say We Rule Like Rabbi Shimon?
Rav Huna Corrects: Meloch Said We Do NOT Rule Like Rabbi Shimon
Rabbi Zeira's Journey to Meloch: Confirming the Authentic Tradition Against Shimon
Final Gemara Ruling: We Do Not Rule Like Rabbi Shimon; Halacha Follows Stricter Standards
Analysis: The Role of Tradition, Sevara, and Practice in Determining Halacha on Lung Defects

macro_segments.title: Forbidden and Permissible Fat (Chelev): Characteristics and Kashering of Organs

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Fat on the Stomach (Keivah) and Whether Kohanim May Eat It—A Question of Vested Interest
Rabbi Yishmael's Position: The Fat on the Keivah Is Not Forbidden Chelev
Siman: Rabbi Yishmael Helps the Kohanim—Evidence from Blessing of the Kohanim
The Debate Over Characteristics: Does Forbidden Chelev Simply Have a Membrane, or Must It Also Cloak?
Resolution: Reversing the First Opinion—Rabbi Akiva Excludes Keivah Fat; Rabbi Yishmael Includes It
Contradiction in Rabbi Yishmael: He Reports His Fathers' View (Lenient) but Personally Disagrees

macro_segments.title: Chelev as a Plug for Holes in Organs: Which Type of Fat Seals Trefah Problems

micro_segments.title: Rav's Rule: Only Permissible Sticky Chelev Fills Holes; Forbidden Chelev Does Not
Rav Sheshet's Lenient Position: Either Type of Chelev Fills Holes
Rav Zeira's Question: What About Chelev of a Deer, Where All Fat Is Permissible?
Answer: The Stickiness of the Fat, Not Its Permissibility Status, Determines If It Plugs Holes

macro_segments.title: Practical Cases: Holes Filled with Forbidden Chelev and Concern for Financial Loss

micro_segments.title: A Hole Filled with Forbidden Chelev Comes Before Rava: Does It Still Render the Animal Kosher?

Rav Pappa's Challenge: Cannot Use Financial Loss Reasoning for Biblical Prohibitions and Serious Doubts
Case: Uncovered Honey and Risk of Snake Poison—A Similar Debate About Financial Loss vs. Danger
Rav Nachman's Counter: Rabbi Shimon Forbids Honey; Life Safety Overrides Monetary Considerations

Final Resolution: At Least We Should Rule Strict on Honey Due to Rabbi Shimon Ben Elazar's Opinion
Analysis: When Does Financial Loss Override Stringency, and When Does Danger Override Leniency?
